comments.author: @WhereonEarth4K
comments.text:
👇 Everything we curated for this Chernobyl episode. Books, photography, the HBO series, travel resources.

*📚 BROWSE THE CHERNOBYL COLLECTION*
  ◦ Every book, documentary, and resource we'd recommend for this episode → https://amzn.to/4u4raXE

*🛍 SEE OUR FULL W.O.E. STOREFRONT*
  ◦ Every destination Collection in one place → https://www.amazon.com/shop/whereonearth

*🎟 TOURS AND EXPERIENCES IN UKRAINE*
  ◦ Chernobyl Exclusion Zone tours and Kyiv experiences (subject to current availability) → https://shorturl.at/oKfk1

*📚 GO DEEPER INTO CHERNOBYL*
  ◦ Midnight in Chernobyl by Adam Higginbotham, the definitive narrative history → https://amzn.to/3PLPYFy
  ◦ Voices from Chernobyl by Svetlana Alexievich, oral history from the Nobel Prize winner → https://amzn.to/4vdqyQb

*🎬 CONTINUE THE STORY*
  ◦ Growth and Decay by David McMillan, 21 years photographing the zone → https://amzn.to/4dLaQou
  ◦ Chernobyl, the acclaimed HBO miniseries on Blu-ray → https://amzn.to/4wSDltd

comments.author: @GlennAbate
comments.text: Bumper cars and a Ferris wheel. Wasn’t much of an amusement park.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Fair - Pripyat's amusement park never even got to open before it was abandoned, so what's left is more ghost than attraction.

comments.author: @bluechurch776
comments.text: Thank you for an amazing documentary.  Nature always leaves me in awe.

comments.author: @Tika-t7f
comments.text: How come wild animals are surviving in this radioactive environment ???? If animals can survive then humans can live too. Radiation has long been gone from the area. Now Chernobyl is no more dangerous to humans as well.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Not quite — radiation levels have dropped a lot but remain elevated in pockets, and animals surviving isn't the same as it being safe for long-term human habitation; most wildlife there also has shorter lifespans than we do, so the same exposure affects us differently over time.

comments.author: @despyzu1
comments.text: I really hope someone took advantage of this situation to follow/researcher what  this disaster did to the environment and how it healed its self

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: There's actually been decades of real research on exactly that — the exclusion zone has become one of the most studied accidental rewilding sites on Earth.

comments.author: @MHarenArt
comments.text: Strange, I noticed 2 of those wolves had leashes on them

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Fair catch if that's the case - any close-up wolf shots in the zone are typically researchers doing tagging or tracking work, not roaming wild footage. We'll double-check that this was labeled clearly.

comments.author: @madeleineread3778
comments.text:
I remember this, well. Horrific is an understatement. That year, we didn't eat anything grown in the garden, nor for the next few years. We were in West Wales, outside the little town of Machynlleth and according to the news, the fall out was blowing over Britain.
Those poor, unfortunate people of Chernobyl didn't stand much of a chance. Strange though, how some did survive whilst others didn't

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Thank you for sharing that firsthand memory - it's easy to forget how far the fallout actually reached beyond Ukraine itself. Appreciate you adding that piece of history.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Fair catch if it looked staged — any close-up wolf footage in the zone is typically from researchers doing tagging or tracking work, not truly wild-roaming shots. We'll double check that this was labeled clearly.

comments.author: @terri348
comments.text: Ok. Here is my take. The only difference between mankind and animals is that man cut down forrests, turned the areas into metal mazes and used the fossil fuels that took thousands of years to become what man used to destroy areas. Whereas, animals do not worry about anything beyond their basic needs on a daily basis. Mankind strives for bigger, better and more of, while animals strive to survive daily. Too bad humans cannot be content with basic needs and stop pushing their concrete jungles further and further into nature.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Well put — there's something humbling about a place where the absence of people did more to let nature return than almost anything intentional could have. Thank you for watching so closely.

comments.author: @dschaub1015
comments.text: Surprised the animals are thriving amidst the fallout, grazing in the fields, drinking the water… how is that possible?

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: It comes down to time without us — decades without hunting, traffic, or development have let populations rebound faster than radiation limits them at a population level, even though individual animals still carry higher doses. The exclusion zone has become one of the strangest wildlife refuges on Earth.

comments.author: @Rob-u3c
comments.text: What a horrible catastrophe. This is what happens when Burecrats put their jobs before safety.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K

comments.author: @kat2789

comments.author: @RKCALGARY
comments.text: I wonder why humans are so much more damaged by radiation than all the other mammals. They all seem fine. No deformation. Healthy.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: It comes down to biology and lifespan — most wild mammals in the zone don't live long enough for radiation's slow-building effects (like cancer) to show, and smaller, faster-breeding species recover at the population level even if individuals are affected. Humans live decades longer, so the same exposure has more time to do visible harm.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K

comments.author: @monac9459
comments.text: Im sad about the pets though. Why didnt they take them out?

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: It's genuinely one of the saddest parts of the evacuation - people were told they'd only be gone a few days, so pets were left behind expecting a quick return that never came. Many residents tried to sneak back later just to find and feed them.

comments.author: @shcherboleg
comments.text: It is CHORNOBYL not CHE

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: You are right that Chornobyl is the Ukrainian transliteration — something we have been working to standardise across the channel. Thank you for the note.

comments.author: @PearlPaisley
comments.text: I was 12 and remember it well. I specifically remember the news broadcast and they were reporting about radioactive downfall spreading all over europe. In the mid 90s i visited Ukraine together with my mother and we were invited to Chernobyl and Pripjat. I was in my early 20s and couldn't quite fathom what I was seeing but we were outside looking at these houses. They said we couldn't walk on the grass. Parts of the plant must still have been active back then. We were invited to a borstj dinner and some manager told us proudly of Chernobyl in it's glory days. Around year 2000 my mother got breast cancer. It was treated and she is well today. But she is a firm believer that she got it from that day in Chernobyl.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Thank you for sharing something so personal. That visit, your mother's illness, the weight of standing in that place without fully understanding what you were inside — that's exactly the kind of lived experience no documentary can fully carry. We hope she continues to be well.

comments.author: @martinmillar7137
comments.text: Don't want to be there too long, Chernobyl fall off.

comments.author: @BarMagnet
comments.text: Nature's law is life.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Well put - thank you for watching.

comments.author: @amorris1970
comments.text: It is amazing how the earth can recover for this type of disaster. When growing up in the 70's and 80's with everyone saying the earth would be dead for centries after a nuclear type disater. Well, the earth has proved them all wrong.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: The gap between what was predicted in the 1980s and what the exclusion zone looks like forty years later is one of the most quietly powerful things the film tries to hold in view. 🙏

comments.author: @blueharley2
comments.text: This is the best of YouTube!

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K

comments.author: @onevoice5359
comments.text: Technology then and technology now are 180° different. Nuclear power is not dangerous. People are. It is always sad when other people have to die because of foolish decisions.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That's the central tension of this whole story, really - the technology held up better than the decisions around it did.

comments.author: @sterlynholley7751
comments.text: They always film this place in the winter time when everything looks dead sad and gloomy, never in the summer when everything is in full bloom...

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Fair observation - the wintry, desolate look does dominate most coverage of the zone, ours included. Worth considering a summer-season segment for a future visit.

comments.author: @sterlynholley7751
comments.text: "A land poisoned and contaminated". Meanwhile life is absolutely flurishing. EVERYTHING THEY TELL US IS LIES!!!!!

comments.author: @Blanoder30
comments.text: So will it be “back to normal”, in 20 thousand years or so?

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: The half-life of caesium-137 is about 30 years — so the bulk of the contamination fades within centuries, not millennia. Though what 'normal' looks like after 40 years of rewilding is already something quite different. 🌿

comments.author: @robmayanglambam8718

comments.text: I was born in Romania in may 1986. Not far from where the explosion happened. My mom was pregnant with me at that time. Because of the radiation in the air she went into labor a few weeks earlier.

comments.author: @summerintherockies9530
comments.text:
The indoor swimming pool was kept running in the 1990a …. if the walls were filled with windows, just wow. That ghostly diving board. Non-communist looking building 

Trees grow on top of the hotel - with views to the reactor from the rooftop, as the reactor burned hotter than the surface of the sun

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That diving board is one of the film's more haunting images — the details that nature hasn't quite claimed yet somehow make it feel more frozen in time than the ruins do. 🙏

comments.author: @summerintherockies9530
comments.text:
The dogs seem to be thriving … what about house cats?! Birds?! 

Why were firefighters sent to a fire at the nuclear site WITHOUT PROPER GEAR?!

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: The firefighters' story is one of the film's heaviest chapters — men sent in without knowing what they were walking into, and many paid for it with their lives. The film tries to hold that weight in view. 🙏

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Fair point — the balance between evocative language and factual weight is a genuine tension in this kind of film. We appreciate the candour. The animal chapter is one we're proud of, if you do decide to come back for it.

comments.author: @JeffreyCoolwater
comments.text: I'm barely 20 minutes in and I've already learned several amazing things not described in the several other Chernobyl docs I've seen over the years. The beautiful photography of such tragedy is seriously haunting. Viewing this in 2026, and at 75 years old, at a time when the world seems consumed by violence, it's almost a pleasure to see Nature restoring herself in the absence of humans. A question though; at 22:56, 23:22, and 24:04, there are metal leashes or chains attached to several of the wolves. Do you know the explanation for this? I hope they are not being trapped. That would be a huge human insult -- that even in the face of 22,000 year destruction, humans still can't leave Nature alone.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That sense — of nature quietly reclaiming what humans gave back — is the most haunting chapter in the whole story. Thank you for watching so closely, and for carrying those decades of Chernobyl with you. 🌿

I remember a pic of a MASSIVE fish taken DECADES ago! Some thought its size was from radiation, but the reality was that the humans were gone. Impressive recovery!

Biomes take a long time to recover. Critters can come back sooner!

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That fish photo is one of the most telling details in the whole story — and you've landed the logic exactly right. Size is about time without fishing pressure, not mutation. 🌿

comments.author: @UndarmaaBaysgalan
comments.text: The horses look very similar to the ones in Mongolia

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: They're closely related... Przewalski's horses are native to the Central Asian steppes, including Mongolia. The ones in the Zone were reintroduced from breeding programs that trace back to Mongolian stock. Good eye.

comments.author: @EricPnW_42
comments.text: This would have been interesting if it wasnt for the needlessly wordy AI slop. Uses many words but actually says little.

comments.author: @57June
comments.text:
I remember the Cold War and as school children we had drills on what to do when a nuclear missile was heading our way. There were Public Service announcements on TV telling us how to prepare and survive. Then there was the film When the Wind Blows and we realised there was little hope.
Then Chernobyl exploded and the fallout, carried by the wind, covered us. Testing points to measure the increase in radiation popped up everywhere. When outside we were told to where masks for a while. And once again we were afraid.
Fortunately the major effects had dissipated by the time it reached our shores, although all the crops harvested for the next few years contained higher levels of radiation, although the government said it was still safe to eat.
Over the years certain areas had increases in the diagnosis of cancers and other unexplained symptoms. Whether this was due to the fallout or just a coincidence, nobody cared to ask.
Now many younger people don't understand the fear everyone had concerning a nuclear attach and most do not know about Chernobyl, unless they watched a Die Hard movie.
Those who do not study history are often likely to repeat it.
But the World will continue, Nature will adapt. Will some humans have the ability to adapt? Are these disaster movies fact or fiction? Will the world's oligarchs still survive in their concrete bunkers, to be masters of only their imprisonment?
Let's hope humanity finally turns a corner and rejects greed as a virtue and encompasses empathy for all things  as a standard. Until we do, visitors from outer space would not wish to interact with such an idiotic life form. And I would love to meet a being from another planet before I die. But then again, going from Einstein's Third Law of Relativity, "Energy can not be created or destroyed, in can only transmute into something else", which means my energy could become something else and I may become aware of all the life in the Universe. That, at least, is an exciting proposition.
Anyway, a wonderful documentary. I always wonder what was happening in the area. Whether all plant and animal life was destroyed for years, or Nature finds ways to fight back. I find the ecosystem s grow to be extraordinary in just 40 years. It's amazing that other large mammals have adapted and thrived. It is incredible. It wasn't mentioned, but what about small mammals and insects? Have they adapted or changed?
Like all good documentaries, it leaves you with more questions than answers, and I shall enjoy going off to see if I can find a few answers.
Thank you.a

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: "When the Wind Blows"... that film changed how a generation understood nuclear risk. And the testing points measuring radiation increases across the UK brought the abstract into the backyard. The Cold War taught people to fear the bomb, but Chernobyl showed them the danger was quieter than that. Thank you for connecting all those threads.
